scope: Comparison against Hermes Agent, NemoClaw, and OpenClaw for system prompt governance, tools, skills, memory, sandboxing, Telegram, and operator control-plane readiness.

statusDefinitions.ahead: ElyzeLabs has stronger current behavior with local evidence and verification.
statusDefinitions.parity: ElyzeLabs has comparable behavior with local evidence and verification.
statusDefinitions.partial: ElyzeLabs has meaningful implementation, but remaining gaps prevent a best-in-class claim.
statusDefinitions.missing: The capability is not implemented in a product-grade way.
statusDefinitions.defer: The capability is intentionally not part of the current best-in-class bar.

capabilities.requirement: Prompts must declare authority order, quote untrusted transcript/memory evidence, scan suspicious context files, and expose prompt assembly metadata to runtime and Doctor surfaces.
referenceSignals: Hermes separates stable prompt policy, context files, memory, and session state.
OpenClaw exposes pluggable context and memory prompt-section contracts.
NemoClaw keeps managed tool selection explicit in onboarding paths.

capabilities.requirement: Durable memory must reject credential-like or prompt-control writes before markdown, daily, structured, imported, or compacted trajectory persistence.
referenceSignals: Hermes treats durable memory as declarative facts rather than instruction authority.
OpenClaw allows memory providers to contribute bounded prompt sections.
NemoClaw keeps credentials outside worker execution boundaries.

capabilities.requirement: Telegram must support governed ingress, prompt-injection-safe memory behavior, bot identity validation, and an operator-triggered outbound smoke test with redacted evidence.
referenceSignals: NemoClaw soft-fails optional Telegram reachability instead of blocking unrelated setup.
OpenClaw doctor/onboarding surfaces risky channel and pairing policy.
Hermes treats channel delivery as a first-class operational concern.
remainingGaps: A live operator-run smoke test with real Telegram credentials still needs to be executed outside mocked integration tests.

capabilities.requirement: Execution policy must expose understandable sandbox tiers, enforce network/filesystem/process/credential boundaries, and preserve approval evidence across all execution lanes.
referenceSignals: NemoClaw defines restricted, balanced, and open policy profiles with endpoint groups.
NemoClaw separates host credentials from sandbox egress.
OpenClaw device permissions and scopes make runtime authority visible.

commands: pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.config.ts packages/gateway/test/unit/sandbox-policy.test.ts packages/config/test/unit/config.test.ts packages/gateway/test/integration/doctor-center.test.ts packages/runtime/test/unit/runtime-manager.test.ts

capabilities.requirement: Tools and skills must have explicit authority boundaries, lifecycle states, review/curation, usage evidence, and conflict/permission diagnostics.
referenceSignals: Hermes has a skill curator loop that pins, archives, consolidates, patches, and reviews skills.
Hermes cron paths disable protected toolsets and scan loaded skill content.
OpenClaw plugin SDK registration clarifies provider/channel/tool extension boundaries.

capabilities.requirement: The control-plane API surface must be discoverable, documented, and drift-checked before new features are claimed as productized.
referenceSignals: OpenClaw documents gateway protocols and channel routing contracts.
NemoClaw ships schemas for sandbox policies and blueprint lifecycle.
Hermes keeps operational CLI/TUI behavior explicit.

capabilities.requirement: Channels should share a deterministic adapter contract for account, peer, thread, agent, session key, pairing policy, inbound normalization, outbound delivery, and audit.
referenceSignals: OpenClaw has deterministic account/peer/guild/channel/agent/session routing.
Hermes supports many channel backends through extension-style platform handling.
OpenClaw pairs unknown DMs before processing.

capabilities.requirement: Browser automation should have agent-only managed profiles, optional user-profile attach, deterministic tab/action controls, artifact preview, and policy-diff approvals.
referenceSignals: OpenClaw separates agent-managed browser profiles from real-user Chrome profile attachment.
OpenClaw documents deterministic tab/click/type/screenshot/PDF browser tools.

commands: pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.config.ts packages/gateway/test/unit/browser-service.test.ts packages/gateway/test/integration/browser-api.test.ts packages/gateway/test/integration/browser-managed-profiles.test.ts dashboard/src/pages/BrowserPage.test.tsx

capabilities.requirement: LLM routing should support provider auth profiles, fallback chains, cooldowns, session stickiness, billing-disabled state, and audit explaining selected model/profile.
referenceSignals: OpenClaw documents auth profile rotation, model fallback, round-robin, session stickiness, cooldowns, and billing-disable behavior.

capabilities.requirement: A typed WebSocket device/node protocol should exist only after route/module boundaries are cleaner.
referenceSignals: OpenClaw has a gateway device/node protocol with handshake, roles, scopes, capabilities, and device tokens.
remainingGaps: Current SSE/event surfaces should be stabilized before adding a node protocol.
Device token lifecycle is not yet implemented.
